# Product brief: AI-assisted onboarding for new users

**Generated by:** ideate skill (product-thinking discussion)
**Date:** 2026-05-17
**Status:** product brief — not yet grounded against a real codebase
or architecture. The next phase reconciles this with project reality.

> This is an example product brief. Real briefs will reflect the
> actual discussion. Use this as a reference for the expected shape,
> not a template to copy. Note that this is intentionally less
> prescriptive than a spec — many details are left for the grounding
> phase.

## How to use this file

Hand this to a coding agent inside the project repo that uses the
agent-project-template. The agent should:

1. Read this file end to end. Treat it as **product intent**, not
   feature requirements. Many things here are deliberately not
   prescriptive.
2. Reconcile it against the project's `.agent/CONTEXT.md` and
   `.agent/ARCHITECTURE.md`. Surface conflicts to the user before
   acting — do NOT silently translate intent into implementation
   choices.
3. Use the `grill-spec` skill (Path B) to convert this brief into
   a structured spec under `.agent/specs/NNNN-x/`. The conversion
   will require the user to make decisions that this brief
   deliberately left open.
4. Treat the "Horizon" section as architectural guidance — what this
   spec is *for* in the longer arc, not just what it does. That
   matters when implementation choices arise.

## Original input

> "Our new users hit a blank dashboard and bounce. I'm thinking
> about a welcome screen of some kind — but I'm not sure if that's
> the right move or whether we should think bigger."

## Why this, why now

Activation has dropped 24 points since the redesign and the gap is
concentrated in the first 5 minutes after signup. The cheap fix
(welcome screen) treats the symptom; the more interesting move is
to make first-session orientation feel like a conversation rather
than a tour. LLMs make that newly affordable. The team has the
technical pieces (auth, role data, an existing agent infrastructure)
already in place — the missing piece is product intent.

## Problem

**Who:** First-time users in the 5 minutes after signup.
**When:** Immediately after the signup form submits and the user
lands on the app for the first time.
**Today:** Users are redirected to a dashboard that's empty for new
accounts. They scroll, don't see obvious next steps, and many leave
without taking any action.
**Cost:** Activation rate (first meaningful action within 24h) is
~38%, down from ~62% pre-redesign. Bounce-within-5-minutes is ~45%
for new users. At current signup volume that's roughly $X/month in
LTV walked away.
**Evidence:** Mixpanel funnel data plus 6 user interviews where the
same pattern came up unprompted.

## Prior art and benchmarks considered

- **Linear's onboarding (2023 redesign):** chose a guided
  in-product flow over a tour. Reported activation improvement of
  ~15–20 points. Strong signal that conversational orientation
  beats static. (Discussed in their public engineering blog.)
- **Notion's "for you" first session:** AI-generated starter
  content tailored to the workspace type chosen at signup. Notable
  because it sidesteps the "blank state" problem entirely by making
  the first state non-blank.
- **Superhuman's white-glove onboarding:** humans, not AI, but
  worth mentioning because it set the bar for "feels like the
  product cares about me showing up". The AI version of this is the
  ambitious framing we discussed.
- **Internal failure case:** the team shipped a static welcome tour
  18 months ago and removed it 3 months later — completion rate was
  ~12% and post-removal metrics didn't change. Strong evidence that
  *static* orientation isn't the answer; the team has already tested
  that.

## Shape (intent, not specification)

What the user experiences (in product terms):

- After signup, the user lands in what feels like a conversation,
  not a dashboard. An assistant introduces itself, asks one or two
  light questions about what they came here to do, and immediately
  helps them do it.
- The conversation produces *real artifacts in the user's account* —
  a created project, a populated list, a saved query — not just
  guidance to go do it themselves later.
- After the first artifact is created, the assistant fades into the
  background. Re-summoning is possible but not pushy.
- Total time from signup to first real artifact: target under 90
  seconds for the median user.

Roughly in scope: the conversational entry point; one or two
"create something real" actions; role-aware framing.

Deliberately not in v1: ongoing AI assistance throughout the
product; voice input; multi-session memory; team onboarding (this
is for individuals).

This section is intentionally less detailed than a spec. The
grounding phase will translate this into testable behavior.

## Innovation framing

The novelty is not "we use AI for onboarding" — many products do.
The leverage is in **what the AI does**: it creates real artifacts
on the user's behalf during the first session, so the user's
account is non-empty by the time they're paying attention to it.
This sidesteps the empty-state problem rather than papering over
it.

There's also a defensibility angle worth flagging: this becomes
better the more the product knows about how role-X users typically
get started, which is data only this team has. The system improves
with usage in a way a third-party tool couldn't replicate.

## Horizon

**What this enables next:**
- A "concierge mode" for stuck users at any point in the lifecycle
  (not just onboarding) — same agent infrastructure, different
  triggers
- Personalized weekly summaries / nudges using the same conversational
  surface
- Plays well with the upcoming integrations roadmap: the agent can
  set up integrations during onboarding when the user mentions them

**What this forecloses or makes harder:**
- A static-tour-based onboarding path becomes confusing to maintain
  alongside this. The team should commit to the conversational
  direction or not — not both.
- If the agent surface ships in onboarding and is poor, it
  poisons users' expectations of AI features elsewhere in the
  product. The bar for v1 is therefore higher than "ships and
  doesn't crash".

**Foundation or leaf:** Foundation. The agent infrastructure used
here will be reused for other use cases; the onboarding flow is
the first beneficiary, not the only one. Worth investing
accordingly.

## Risks and assumptions

- **Load-bearing assumption:** users want conversation, not
  efficiency. Some user segments will find it slower than just
  clicking through a dashboard. Worth validating with a small
  cohort before broad rollout.
- **Technical risk:** latency. If first agent response takes >4
  seconds, the "feels alive" effect dies. Streaming responses
  partly mitigates; cold-start times don't.
- **Quality risk:** the agent will sometimes get the user's intent
  wrong. Recovery (the user redirects, the agent gracefully
  adjusts) matters more than first-shot accuracy.
- **Market risk:** the bar is rising fast. By the time this ships,
  several competitors will likely have shipped similar things.
  Doesn't kill the idea, but the bar for "good enough" will be
  higher than current benchmarks.
- **Cheap falsification:** a Wizard-of-Oz test with 10–20 new users
  (a human plays the agent) would tell us whether the *concept*
  works before we build anything.

## Success signals (qualitative)

How we'll know this is working — specific metrics come later:

- New users have at least one meaningful artifact in their account
  by the end of session 1 (significantly more than today)
- Activation rate (24h meaningful action) trends back toward
  pre-redesign levels
- Qualitative: in user interviews, new users describe the product
  as "helpful" or "smart" rather than "confusing" or "empty"
- Negative signal to watch: users feeling the agent is intrusive,
  paternalistic, or slow

## Constraints worth flagging

- Privacy: the agent sees what users type during onboarding. This
  is logged for analysis. Compliance with the existing data
  handling policy is required.
- Accessibility: any conversational UI needs keyboard navigation,
  screen reader support, and a non-conversational fallback path.
- Latency: see Risks. First response under 2s p50, under 4s p95.
- Cost: per-session LLM cost should be modeled before launch.
  Probably modest given session length, but worth knowing.

## Open decisions

Deliberately left unresolved for the grounding phase:

- Which agent framework powers this (LangGraph, Pydantic-AI, or
  custom). The team already has experience with both; pick based
  on existing patterns.
- Whether the conversational surface is a modal, a dedicated
  route, or a persistent side panel. Each has UX trade-offs that
  the grounding phase should weigh against the rest of the app.
- Exact failure UX when the agent is down or rate-limited — needs
  a designed fallback path, not just "an error".
- How dismissable the agent is. Probably "very" — but the exact
  affordance is design work.

## Discussion notes

For posterity — angles considered, alternatives explored, key
things the user reacted strongly to:

- Initially the user framed this as "welcome screen v2". We
  pressure-tested that against the team's prior welcome-tour failure
  and concluded the static-orientation pattern is exhausted for this
  product. The conversational framing emerged from that pivot.
- Considered the "make the dashboard non-empty by default"
  approach (Notion-style) — fill the new user's account with
  sample data. Rejected because the team's product doesn't have
  obvious "sample" semantics; users would have to delete the
  samples before doing real work.
- The 10x version we discussed: agent as ongoing companion, not
  just onboarding. Out of scope for this brief, but the foundation
  framing in Horizon was chosen specifically to make that direction
  feasible later.
- User pushed back on the "humans not AI" Superhuman comparison,
  pointing out their team doesn't have the headcount for white-glove.
  Fair. The conversational AI approach is partly a way to get the
  Superhuman feeling at SaaS scale.
- The Wizard-of-Oz test came up late in the discussion and the
  user got visibly more interested. Worth surfacing in grounding —
  may want to gate the engineering investment on the test
  succeeding.
